name: Boats and Streams — Going With and Against the Flow
whatIsIt: A boat has its own speed in still water; a river adds or subtracts its current. DOWNSTREAM (with the flow) the boat goes faster: boat + stream. UPSTREAM (against the flow) it goes slower: boat − stream. From the two, you recover both hidden speeds: boat speed = (down + up) ÷ 2, and stream speed = (down − up) ÷ 2. It's the same idea whenever something helps or fights motion — a plane with tail/head wind, a walker on a moving escalator.
whyNeeded: Rowing, ferries, swimming, planes in wind, and escalators all follow this add/subtract rule. Boats-and-streams is a classic SSC, Bank and Railway aptitude topic, and the two half-sum formulas crack most questions instantly.
realLifeUses: A rower going down a river vs struggling back upstream
A ferry crossing a flowing river
A plane with a tailwind (faster) or headwind (slower)
Walking on a moving airport escalator, with or against it
SSC / Bank / Railway quantitative aptitude questions

vocabulary.meaning: The boat's own speed with no current (what you'd row in a pond).
The river's speed; it adds going downstream, subtracts going upstream.
With the flow — speed = boat + stream (faster).
Against the flow — speed = boat − stream (slower).

story.title: Rowing to the Village and Back
story.text: Latha rows downstream at 12 km/h but crawls back upstream at only 8 km/h. 'The river is playing tricks!' The boatman laughs: 'The river helps you one way and fights you the other. Your true rowing speed is the average of the two — (12 + 8) ÷ 2 = 10 km/h. And the river's speed is half the difference — (12 − 8) ÷ 2 = 2 km/h. Downstream you get 10 + 2 = 12; upstream 10 − 2 = 8. See?' Latha finally understands the river's arithmetic.
story.extractedProblem: A boat goes 12 km/h downstream and 8 km/h upstream. Find the boat's still-water speed and the stream speed.

visual.caption: The current ADDS downstream and SUBTRACTS upstream. Recover the hidden speeds: boat = half the sum, stream = half the difference.

teachingGallery.title: Add going down, subtract going up
teachingGallery.note: Down = b + s, Up = b − s. So b = (down + up)/2 and s = (down − up)/2.

standardMethod.summary: Downstream speed = boat + stream; upstream = boat − stream; solve the pair with the half-sum and half-difference.
steps: Stream speed = (D − U) ÷ 2. (Time = distance ÷ the right speed.)

alternateMethods.name: Forward direction
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When you know boat and stream and need the two travel speeds.
steps: Time = distance ÷ speed for each leg.

workedExamples.problem: A boat rows 15 km/h downstream and 9 km/h upstream. Find the still-water speed.
Same boat (down 15, up 9). Find the stream (current) speed.

trickPractice.trick: Boat = half the sum, stream = half the difference
trickPractice.intro: Given downstream and upstream speeds, average them for the boat and half their gap for the current.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the stream for upstream and subtracting it for downstream (getting the directions backwards).
commonMistakes.fix: The current HELPS you downstream (add) and FIGHTS you upstream (subtract). Downstream must be the FASTER speed. If your 'upstream' came out faster than 'downstream', you swapped them.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using half the SUM for the stream and half the DIFFERENCE for the boat.
commonMistakes.fix: The bigger quantity is the boat: boat = (D + U)/2 (half the sum), stream = (D − U)/2 (half the difference). The stream is always the smaller of the two.

challenge.q: A boat covers 36 km downstream at 12 km/h downstream speed. How many hours?
Which of these is always the larger speed?
hintLadder: A boat must move even with no current.
The stream is only the extra push.
So the boat speed is the bigger one.

teachBackPrompt: Teach a friend the boats rule: downstream adds the current, upstream subtracts it. Use down 12, up 8 and show boat = (12+8)/2 = 10 and stream = (12−8)/2 = 2. Explain why downstream must be the faster speed.

revisionCard.summary: Downstream = boat + stream (faster); Upstream = boat − stream (slower). Boat = (D + U)/2 (half the sum); Stream = (D − U)/2 (half the difference). The boat speed is always larger than the stream. Same add/subtract idea works for wind and escalators. Time = distance ÷ the right speed.

gameMission.title: Row With the River
gameMission.brief: Race up and down the river! Add the current going down, subtract it going up, and recover the hidden boat and stream speeds before the tide turns.

realLifeProject: On an airport escalator (or a treadmill), estimate your normal walking speed, then your speed walking with it and against it. Notice: with = walk + belt, against = walk − belt — exactly boats and streams.

rememberIt.hook: Down adds, Up subtracts; boat = half-sum, stream = half-difference
rememberIt.unpack: D = b + s, U = b − s. b = (D+U)/2, s = (D−U)/2. Boat speed is always the bigger one.

formulas.name: Directional speeds
formulas.formula: D = b + s ,  U = b − s

studentQuestions.a: Because the river's current pushes the boat in the same direction, so its speed adds to the boat's own speed.
Yes — a tailwind adds to the plane's speed (like downstream) and a headwind subtracts (like upstream).
